request.question: Whose bestseller list did the author of Empress Bianca have a biography on in 1992?

title: Empress Bianca
The Secrets of Sir Richard Kenworthy
Beth Wiseman
List of Fear Street books
Vision in White
Karen Rose Smith
Rumour Has It (novel)
Christopher Scheer
The Te of Piglet
Lady Colin Campbell
sentences: Empress Bianca, the first novel by Lady Colin Campbell, was initially published in June 2005.

One month later, Arcadia Books, the British publisher, withdrew the book and pulped all unsold copies in reaction to a legal threat initiated on behalf of Lily Safra under her interpretation that the book was a defamatory roman à clef.
After some changes the book was republished in the United States in 2008 by Dynasty Press.
The Secrets of Sir Richard Kenworthy is a Regency romance written by Julia Quinn and published in 2015 by Avon Books.
Quinn's 24th novel is also the final installment of her series known as the Smythe-Smith Quartet.
It features Iris Smythe-Smith and the eponymous Sir Richard Kenworthy.
The novel reached number 2 on the New York Times Bestseller List and was in the top 10 for the "USA Today" bestseller list.
Beth Wiseman is the best-selling author of the "Daughters of the Promise" series and the "Land of Canaan" series.
Having sold over 1.5 million books, her novels have held spots on the ECPA (Evangelical Christian Publishers Association) Bestseller List and the CBA (Christian Book Association) Bestseller List.
She was the recipient of the prestigious Carol Award in 2011 and 2013.
She is a three-time winner of the Inspirational Readers Choice Award, and an INSPY Award winner.
In 2013 she won the coveted Holt Medallion.
Her first book in the "Land of Canaan" series — "Seek Me With All Your Heart" — was selected as the 2011 Women of Faith Book of the Year.
Wiseman is contracted with HarperCollins Christian Publishing into the year 2018, and she has published thirteen novels and thirteen novellas to date.
This is a list of books from the "Fear Street" book series created and written by R. L. Stine.
The first book, "The New Girl" was published in 1989.
Various spin-off series were written, including the "Fear Street Sagas" and "Ghosts of Fear Street".
More than 80 million "Fear Street" books have been sold as of 2003.

The books appeared in many bestseller lists, including the "New York Times" Best Seller list for children, "USA Today" bestseller list and "Publishers Weekly" bestseller list, and the series was listed as the bestselling young-adult book series of all time.
Vision In White is the first book of the Bride Quartet series of romance novels, written by Nora Roberts.

It spent two weeks atop the "New York Times" Bestseller List and reached number 3 on the "USA Today" bestseller list, marking the first time one of Roberts' books had become a bestseller in trade paperback format.
A downloadable casual-play computer game based on the book was introduced by I-Play in 2010.
Karen Rose Smith was born in Pennsylvania.
Her first romance was published in 1992; her 97th novel, a mystery, will be published in 2017.
It is the sixth in her eight book Caprice De Luca Home Staging mystery series.
Her Daisy's Tea Garden mystery series will begin in 2018.
She has written for Meteor/Kismet, Kensington, Silhouette and Harlequin.
She has also indie-published novels and short story collections in e-book format.

Twice a winner of New Jersey's Golden Leaf Award in Short Contemporary Romance, she has also been honored with CRA's Award Of Excellence for short contemporary, as well as the Golden Quill for Traditional Romance.
Her romances have made the USA TODAY list and Amazon's romance bestseller list.
Her mystery, Staged To Death, climbed to #1 on Amazon's cozy mystery bestseller list.
Married, she spend her days writing, gardening, cooking and keeping her four rescued cats company.
Rumour Has It is a novel by British author Jill Mansell.
Rumour Has It, spent eight weeks in The Sunday Times hardback bestseller list in 2009 and the paperback ranked third in The Sunday Times bestseller list

Christopher Scheer (born September 8, 1968) is the co-author, with Robert Scheer and Lakshmi Chaudhry, of "The Five Biggest Lies Bush Told Us About Iraq", published in 2003 in the U.S., England and Australia.
The book appeared on the "Los Angeles Times" bestseller list and was a part of the national debate in 2004 about the then still popular Iraq War.
In 2010, he received co-author credit, with his father, on "The Great American Stickup", which also appeared on the "Los Angeles Times" bestseller list.
In 2016, he received co-author credit for "," with Narda Zacchino.
The Te of Piglet is a 1992 philosophical non-fiction book written by Benjamin Hoff as a companion to his 1982 work "The Tao of Pooh".
The book was published by Dutton Books and spent 21 weeks on the "Publishers Weekly" Bestseller List and 37 weeks on the "New York Times" Bestseller List.
Georgia Arianna, Lady Colin Campbell ("née" Ziadie; born 17 August 1949) is a Jamaican-born British writer, socialite, and television and radio personality.
She has published three books about the British Royal Family.
They include biographies of Diana, Princess of Wales, which was on "The New York Times" bestseller list in 1992, and Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother.
She has also written two autobiographies, one exploring her mother.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Whose bestseller list did the author of Empress Bianca have a biography on in 1992?
hop1_docs: Bianca Bosker | Bianca Bosker is an American journalist and author whose "New York Times" bestselling book "Cork Dork" has been reviewed by "The New York Times" and "Publishers Weekly".

Jen Lancaster | Jennifer ("Jen") Lancaster (born November 5, 1967) is an American author whose titles have appeared on the New York Times bestseller list.  As of August 2015, she has twelve published books.

Luiz Cesar Pimentel | Luiz Cesar Pimentel (São Paulo, January 22, 1971) is a Brazilian journalist, writer and currently Director of Content of the Web Portal R7, of Record TV.  He is the author of five books, biographies, that have entered the list of the 20 bestsellers in Brazil by "Veja" Magazine.  He is also a collaborator of some of the main vehicles, newspapers and magazines, in his country such as "Rolling Stone", "Elle" and "Playboy".

James L. Snyder | James Lucion Snyder (born July 21, 1951) is an American writer.  He is a minister with the Christian and Missionary Alliance and an award winning author and humorist whose writings have appeared in more than eighty periodicals including Guideposts.  Snyder's first book, his 1991 biography of A.W. Tozer, "In Pursuit of God: The Life of A. W. Tozer," won the Reader’s Choice Award in 1992 by Christianity Today.  Snyder has authored 15 books altogether.

Carlo Bertinazzi | Carlo Antonio Bertinazzi (2 December 1710, Turin – 6 September 1783), known as Carlin, was an Italian actor and author.  He is known to have traveled with Giacomo Casanova's mother, Zanetta Farussi, to St Petersburg to perform for Empress Anna of Russia, only to return to Italy shortly after, as the empress did not approve of the comedy.

Robert Rankin | Robert Fleming Rankin (born 27 July 1949) is a prolific British author of comedic fantasy novels.  Born in Parsons Green, London, he started writing in the late 1970s, and first entered the bestsellers lists with "Snuff Fiction" in 1999, by which time his previous eighteen books had sold around one million copies.  His books are a mix of science fiction, fantasy, the occult, urban legends, running gags, metafiction, steampunk and outrageous characters.  According to the (largely fictional) biography printed in some Corgi editions of his books, Rankin refers to his style as 'Far Fetched Fiction' in the hope that bookshops will let him have a section to himself.  Many of Rankin's books are bestsellers.

Gabriel Sherman | Gabriel Sherman is an American author.  In 2014, he wrote a biography about Fox News Channel president Roger Ailes called "The Loudest Voice in the Room: How the Brilliant, Bombastic Roger Ailes Built Fox News – and Divided a Country", which debuted at #9 on The New York Times Bestseller list.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Bianca Bosker — [1] — '"New York Times" bestselling book "Cork Dork"'
- Jen Lancaster — [2] — 'titles have appeared on the New York Times bestseller list.'
- Luiz Cesar Pimentel — [3] — 'have entered the list of the 20 bestsellers in Brazil by "Veja" Magazine.'
- James L. Snyder — [4] — 'won the Reader’s Choice Award in 1992 by Christianity Today.'
- Carlo Bertinazzi — [5] — 'perform for Empress Anna of Russia,'
- Robert Rankin — [6] — 'first entered the bestsellers lists with "Snuff Fiction" in 1999'
- Gabriel Sherman — [7] — 'debuted at #9 on The New York Times Bestseller list.'

Ambiguities:
- "Empress Bianca" — no passage mentions this title or its author; could be conflated with "Bianca Bosker" or "Empress Anna of Russia" (passage [5]).
- "bestseller list" — multiple lists appear in passages (e.g., 'New York Times bestseller list', 'the 20 bestsellers in Brazil by "Veja" Magazine'); which list is meant is unclear.
- 1992 reference in passages refers to an award (Reader’s Choice Award) not to a bestseller list; no passage shows a biography appearing on a bestseller list in 1992.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing identity of the "author of Empress Bianca" (name of the author not present).
- Missing the fact whether that author had a biography on any bestseller list in 1992, and if so which bestseller list (name) and which biography (title).

Retrieval direction:
1) Search Wikipedia for the work: page title "Empress Bianca" or query: '"Empress Bianca" author' (quoted).
2) If no direct page, try: '"Empress Bianca" book "author" "biography"' (quoted query).
3) Search for combined facts: '"Empress Bianca" author 1992 "bestseller"' (quoted).
4) If Empress Bianca is a person, search disambiguation: 'Empress Bianca (disambiguation)' or query '"Empress Bianca" Wikipedia'.
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Bianca Bosker | Bianca Bosker is an American journalist and author whose "New York Times" bestselling book "Cork Dork" has been reviewed by "The New York Times" and "Publishers Weekly".

Empress Bianca | Empress Bianca, the first novel by Lady Colin Campbell, was initially published in June 2005.  One month later, Arcadia Books, the British publisher, withdrew the book and pulped all unsold copies in reaction to a legal threat initiated on behalf of Lily Safra under her interpretation that the book was a defamatory roman à clef.  After some changes the book was republished in the United States in 2008 by Dynasty Press.

Bianca Maria Sforza | Bianca Maria Sforza (5 April 1472 – 31 December 1510) was a Queen of the Romans and Holy Roman Empress as the second spouse of Maximilian I, Holy Roman Emperor.  She was the eldest legitimate daughter of Galeazzo Maria Sforza, Duke of Milan, by his second wife, Bona of Savoy.

Evernight (series) | Evernight is a series of five vampire-based romantic fantasy novels by "New York Times" bestselling American author Claudia Gray.  It tells the story of Bianca Olivier, a 16-year-old half-vampire girl born to two vampires, who is forced to attend Evernight Academy, a private boarding school for vampires (although some humans are enrolled).  She was enrolled in order to fulfill her destiny to become a full vampire, even though she feels she doesn't belong there.  Bianca then meets and falls in love with a human named Lucas Ross, who also feels isn't the "Evernight" type, but their love becomes forbidden by their families and friends when the truth of each other's nature comes to light.  Not only is it revealed that Bianca is a vampire, but it is also revealed that Lucas is a member of the ancient vampire hunting group Black Cross.

The Last Empress (novel) | The Last Empress is a historical novel by Anchee Min that provides a sympathetic account of the life of Empress Dowager Cixi (referred to as Empress Orchid), from her rise to power as Empress Tzu-Hsi, until her death at 72 years of age.  Akin to the bestselling and preceding novel in the series Empress Orchid, names within the story are different in spelling but retain the same pronunciation - allowing the reader to identify each relevant character to his or her real life counterpart.

Bianca Zoonekynd | Bianca Elston Zoonekynd or Bianca Budler (born (1992--)14 1992 ) is a South African individual and double-mini trampolinist, representing her nation at international competitions.  She competed at world championships, including at the 2009, 2010, 2011, 2013, 2014 and 2015 Trampoline World Championships, winning the silver medal in the individual double-mini event in 2010 and 2011.

Rumour Has It (novel) | Rumour Has It is a novel by British author Jill Mansell.  Rumour Has It, spent eight weeks in The Sunday Times hardback bestseller list in 2009 and the paperback ranked third in The Sunday Times bestseller list
response.summary_2: No supporting evidence in the provided passages that Lady Colin Campbell (author of Empress Bianca) had a biography on any bestseller list in 1992.
